item.title: CLEAR VISION FOR A BRIGHTER FUTURE | How frequently do we need an eye check-up?
item.description: OUR eyes are very important because they help us see and enjoy the world around us. Many people only visit an eye doctor when they have a problem, but regular eye check-ups are just as important as visits to the dentist or family doctor. They help keep our eyes healthy and can find problems before they become serious. Children should have their eyes checked regularly because their vision is still
item.content:encoded:
OUR eyes are very important because they help us see and enjoy the world around us. Many people only visit an eye doctor when they have a problem, but regular eye check-ups are just as important as visits to the dentist or family doctor. They help keep our eyes healthy and can find problems before they become serious.

Children should have their eyes checked regularly because their vision is still developing. Good eyesight helps them read, write, and learn better in school. If a child has trouble seeing the board or reading a book, an eye check-up can help find the cause and provide the right treatment.

Adults with healthy eyes should usually have an eye check-up every one to two years. However, people who wear glasses or contact lenses may need to visit the eye doctor every year, not just to get new glasses, but to check the health of their eyes, assess their vision with their current glasses or contact lenses, and see whether their prescription has changed. Older adults, especially those over the age of 60, should also have yearly eye exams because the risk of eye diseases increases with age.

Some people need more frequent eye check-ups. This includes people with diabetes, high blood pressure, a family history of eye disease, or those who have had an eye injury. Regular check-ups can help detect conditions such as glaucoma, cataracts, or diabetic eye disease before they affect vision. Early treatment often gives better results.

It is also important to see an eye doctor if you notice changes in your vision. Blurry vision, eye pain, headaches, flashes of light, double vision, or sudden loss of sight should never be ignored. Getting medical help quickly can prevent more serious problems.

There are also simple ways to keep our eyes healthy. We should eat healthy foods, get enough sleep, protect our eyes from strong sunlight, and take regular breaks when using computers, tablets, or mobile phones. Reading in good light and avoiding rubbing our eyes can also help protect our vision. In conclusion, regular eye check-ups are an important part of staying healthy. Even if our eyes feel fine, routine eye exams can find problems early and protect our eyesight. By taking care of our eyes and visiting an eye doctor when needed, we can enjoy clear vision and a better quality of life for many years.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: FCEF | Commercial value of customer feedback
item.description: Every business receives customer feedback. Sometimes it comes as praise, sometimes as a suggestion, and others as a complaint.
item.content:encoded:
Every business receives customer feedback.

Sometimes it comes as praise, sometimes as a suggestion, and others as a complaint.

While many organisations view complaints as a problem to be resolved, successful businesses recognise them for what they really are: valuable commercial intelligence.

In Fiji’s small and closely connected economy, where businesses rely heavily on repeat customers, referrals and community reputation, the way organisations respond to customer feedback can directly influence profitability, productivity and long-term growth.

According to the Consumer Council of Fiji, customer dissatisfaction affects almost every major area of the private sector.

During the 2023–2024 financial year, the Council recorded 958 complaints involving financial scams and fraud, representing 29 per cent of all complaints received.

– 508 complaints relating to food and beverages, including expired products, spoiled food and poor restaurant hygiene;

– 239 online shopping complaints involving delivery, product quality and fraudulent activity;

– 195 faulty household electronic goods complaints;

– 165 complaints relating to electronic and computer services;

– 146 complaints involving VAT, receipts and stamp duties;

– 142 misleading advertising complaints; and

– 127 complaints concerning hardware products.

These figures illustrate that customer concerns are not isolated incidents.

They occur across almost every industry and, if left unresolved, can carry significant commercial consequences.

Businesses that manage customer complaints effectively do far more than resolve an individual issue.

They strengthen customer trust, improve loyalty, identify operational weaknesses and create opportunities for continuous improvement.

In today’s competitive environment, effective complaint management is good business.

International research by Bain & Company claims that customer retention by just 5 per cent can increase profits by between 25 and 95 per cent.

This reinforces the principle – retaining an existing customer is significantly more valuable than replacing one who has been lost through poor service or unresolved complaints.

Closer to home, the Consumer Council of Fiji reported that consumers lodged 4880 complaints valued at more than $FJ5.58 million in a single financial year, with the Council helping recover $FJ3.64 million through refunds, repairs and replacements.

These figures highlight both the financial cost of unresolved issues but also the value of addressing concerns early before they escalate.

Trust itself has become a competitive advantage.

Fiji’s growing digital economy provides a useful example of the relationship between customer experience and business confidence.

A recent Consumer Council survey found that 36 per cent of participants rated the customer service provided by digital financial service providers as average, poor or very poor.

The report noted that ineffective customer support often resulted in frustration, unresolved issues and reluctance to continue using digital platforms.

Even more telling was that 77 per cent of respondents had never lodged a formal complaint with either a service provider or regulator despite experiencing issues.

Reasons included limited awareness of complaint procedures and a belief that raising concerns would not achieve a satisfactory outcome.

For businesses, this is an important reminder that silence should never be mistaken for satisfaction.

Many dissatisfied customers simply leave.

item.title: BASTILLE DAY | The birth of modern France
item.description: EVERY year on July 14, France celebrates Bastille Day, its national holiday, commemorating one of the most significant events in the country’s history the storming of the Bastille prison in Paris in 1789. The day marks the beginning of the French Revolution and symbolises the triumph of liberty, equality, and the people’s fight against oppression.
item.content:encoded:
EVERY year on July 14, France celebrates Bastille Day, its national holiday, commemorating one of the most significant events in the country’s history the storming of the Bastille prison in Paris in 1789.

The day marks the beginning of the French Revolution and symbolises the triumph of liberty, equality, and the people’s fight against oppression.

Originally built in the 14th century as a medieval fortress, the Bastille later became a state prison under the Bourbon monarchy.

According to britannica.com, it housed political prisoners as well as individuals detained by royal order without the right to appeal.

Among its most famous inmates were philosopher Voltaire, the Marquis de Sade, and the mysterious prisoner known as “The Man in the Iron Mask.”

Although prisoners of noble rank often lived in relatively comfortable conditions, the Bastille became a powerful symbol of the monarchy’s absolute authority and the injustices of the old regime.

By the late 18th century, the prison held very few inmates and was even scheduled for demolition.

However, public anger toward the monarchy was reaching a breaking point. On 14 July 1789, a crowd of Parisians marched to the Bastille seeking the weapons and ammunition stored inside.

When the fortress’s defenders resisted, the crowd stormed the prison, capturing it and freeing the seven prisoners being held there.

Although only a handful of prisoners were released, the fall of the Bastille carried enormous symbolic importance.

It marked the collapse of royal power and became the defining moment that launched the French Revolution, paving the way for sweeping political and social change across France and eventually inspiring democratic movements around the world.

More than 90 years later, in 1880, 14 July was officially declared France’s national holiday.

Since then, Bastille Day has been celebrated with patriotic ceremonies, military parades, spectacular fireworks, concerts, and community gatherings.

The famous military parade along the Champs-Élysées in Paris remains one of the largest and most recognised national celebrations in the world.

The slogan “Vive le 14 juillet!” (“Long live the 14th of July!”) continues to capture the spirit of the occasion, celebrating freedom, unity, and national pride.

Bastille Day is also observed in several French overseas territories and former French colonies that maintain close cultural ties with France.

In French Polynesia, for example, the celebrations blend French traditions with local customs, featuring weeks of singing, dancing, drumming, and cultural competitions throughout July.

Beyond France, Francophiles around the world mark the occasion by enjoying French cuisine, attending concerts of French music, participating in cultural festivals, or simply celebrating France’s rich history and heritage.

Today, Bastille Day serves not only as a reminder of a pivotal moment in French history but also as a celebration of the enduring values of liberty, equality, and fraternity that continue to define the French Republic.

Bastille day mass picnic on the Champ de Mars in France. Picture: tripadvisor.com

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: BASTILLE DAY | The historic celebration that shaped modern France
item.description: BASTILLE Day 2026 is not just a fun holiday on the calendar. It is a day that links modern France to an important time from the French revolution. For French people, July 14 is a day full of history, pride, and joy that everyone can see.
item.content:encoded:
BASTILLE Day 2026 is not just a fun holiday on the calendar. It is a day that links modern France to an important time from the French revolution.

For French people, July 14 is a day full of history, pride, and joy that everyone can see.

You will notice the flags, big parades, fireworks, and street parties everywhere.

If you want to know why this day means so much, you need to look at what it stands for.

Understanding Bastille Day: Its Meaning and Significance

Bastille Day is France’s national holiday, and it happens every year on July 14th. It marks a big event of the French Revolution and is a symbol of the end of royal control.

Now, the day is about French culture, public pride, and there is a feeling of shared memory. You will often hear la Marseillaise, see the national colors all around, and watch how the celebration brings the history out for everyone.

To see why people think July 14th is such a big day, first look at what it means to the culture.

The Importance of July 14 in French Culture

For many people in France, July 14 is not just a day to stay home. This is a time when history, memories, and fun all mix together. People fill the streets, city squares, and famous spots with the colors of the French flag.

You will see it and feel it all around you.

But there is also something special about how this day brings the French people together. Families, friends, and people from near and far come out for parades, music, fireworks, and dancing.

That time together is what makes the day feel so strong for everyone, in both big cities and small towns.

Music is also a big part of this day. People hear the French national anthem everywhere. The national anthem is a big link to the past and helps show what the country stands for right now.

This is how July 14th stays a big part of French culture and is a sign of the unity of the French people.

Why Bastille Day Is a National Holiday in France

Bastille Day is a national holiday in France. It marks a big moment in French history.

People talk about the wider story of the Revolution on this day. In France, many call it le quatorze juillet or La Fête Nationale instead of Bastille Day.

The date was made a public holiday in 1880 by French law. This law made the celebration a big part of national life. It shows how the memory of the revolution ties to what people now see in the French republic.

You can notice the official side of it today. On July 14th, businesses and places might change hours.

Communities put together public events for everyone.

The day has legal meaning, and it stands for something too.

That is why July 14th, or le quatorze juillet, is so special in France. Next, let’s look at the story behind it.

The start of Bastille Day goes back to 1789. At that time, the french revolution began to take shape. On July 14th, people in Paris went and attacked the bastille prison.

This prison was a strong building, and it stood for the power of the french monarchy.

France had trouble then. There was not enough food, people were upset, and the country was tense under louis xvi.

The fall of the bastille was more than just about the fortress. The event sent a call that people would not take royal control anymore. The crisis became the french revolution after this attack. In the next sections, you will find out how that change happened.

The French Revolution and the Storming of the Bastille

The French revolution started because there was a lot of unfairness and people were having a hard time. France was in a severe economic crisis, and many people in the country struggled with money.

There were problems, not enough basic things for people, and trouble between classes. All this made people in Paris upset in 1789.

item.title: LOCAL GETAWAYS | Welcome to paradise and discover warmth and hospitality
item.description: FIJI is more than just a tropical paradise it’s a feeling. From the moment you arrive, you’ll be welcomed with a heartfelt “Bula!” and genuine smiles that reflect the warmth and hospitality of the Fijian people. Surrounded by crystal-clear waters, palm-fringed beaches, and vibrant coral reefs, Fiji offers an unforgettable escape for every kind of traveler.
item.content:encoded:
FIJI is more than just a tropical paradise it’s a feeling. From the moment you arrive, you’ll be welcomed with a heartfelt “Bula!” and genuine smiles that reflect the warmth and hospitality of the Fijian people.

Surrounded by crystal-clear waters, palm-fringed beaches, and vibrant coral reefs, Fiji offers an unforgettable escape for every kind of traveler.

According to Outrigger.com, whether you dream of relaxing with a cocktail on a pristine white-sand beach, snorkeling through colorful coral gardens, or exploring lush tropical islands, Fiji has something for everyone.

From luxurious private island retreats to family-friendly beachfront resorts packed with activities, you’ll find the perfect destination to create memories that last a lifetime.

Planning your first trip to Fiji? A little preparation will help ensure your holiday is smooth, enjoyable, and stress-free. Here are a few essential travel tips before you arrive.

The official currency is the Fijian Dollar (FJD). While cash is handy for local markets, taxis, and small village purchases, major credit cards are widely accepted at most resorts, restaurants, and larger shops.

ATMs are conveniently located in towns and at Nadi International Airport.

If you’re planning to visit remote islands or traditional villages, it’s a good idea to carry some cash. Tipping isn’t expected in Fiji, but it is always appreciated when you receive exceptional service.

Many resorts also operate staff Christmas or community funds, allowing guests to contribute in support of local employees.

English is one of Fiji’s official languages, making it easy for international visitors to communicate throughout the islands.

You’ll also hear the beautiful Fijian language spoken by locals, while Hindi is commonly spoken within Indo-Fijian communities.

Learning a few local phrases is a wonderful way to connect with the people:

The famous “Bula Spirit” is central to Fijian culture, so don’t hesitate to greet everyone you meet with a cheerful “Bula!”

Fiji uses Type I electrical outlets, the same as Australia and New Zealand, with a voltage of 240V and a frequency of 50Hz.

Visitors traveling from North America or Europe may need a plug adapter and, for some devices, a voltage converter.

Most resorts provide essential amenities such as hair dryers and charging facilities, so check with your accommodation before packing extra electrical items.

Fiji enjoys warm tropical weather throughout the year, making it an attractive destination in every season.

The dry season, from May to October, offers sunny skies, cooler evenings, and lower humidity.

It’s considered the best time for outdoor adventures, snorkeling, diving, hiking, and relaxing on Fiji’s beautiful beaches.

The wet season, from November to April, brings warmer temperatures, occasional tropical showers, and lush green landscapes.

Although rainfall is more frequent, showers are often brief, and visitors can enjoy fewer crowds and attractive accommodation deals.

Fiji is considered a safe and welcoming destination, but a few simple precautions will help you enjoy your holiday comfortably.

Pack reef-safe sunscreen to protect both your skin and Fiji’s delicate coral ecosystems.

If you’re visiting during the wetter months, insect repellent is recommended to protect against mosquitoes. Many resorts also provide mosquito nets or repellents for guests.

When staying in remote areas, bottled or filtered drinking water is advisable, although most established resorts provide treated water that is safe to drink.

Comprehensive travel insurance is strongly recommended, particularly if you plan to enjoy activities such as scuba diving, surfing, sailing, or island hopping.

Insurance can also provide peace of mind for unexpected travel delays or medical emergencies.

One of Fiji’s greatest treasures is its rich cultural heritage. Visitors are encouraged to embrace local customs and traditions as a sign of respect.

If you’re invited to visit a traditional village, remove your hat and sunglasses before entering, and dress modestly, especially during ceremonies or church visits.

Should you be welcomed with a traditional kava ceremony, it’s polite to accept the cup with both hands before taking a sip.

item.title: POINT OF ORIGIN I 100 years on – A symbol of resilience, trust and sacrifice
item.description: STANDING proudly on the waterfront at Wairiki in Taveuni, Meridian Store has become more than just a place of business. For a century, it has served generations of island families, weathered changing times and remained a symbol of resilience built on sacrifice, determination and community trust.
item.content:encoded:
STANDING proudly on the waterfront at Wairiki in Taveuni, Meridian Store has become more than just a place of business.

For a century, it has served generations of island families, weathered changing times and remained a symbol of resilience built on sacrifice, determination and community trust.

As the store marked its 100th anniversary, managing director Champak Lal Dayaram said the occasion was about much more than celebrating a successful business.

“It is about remembering the journey of one man who left everything behind to search for opportunities in a country he knew nothing about,” he said.

Mr Dayaram told guests that the story began in 1885 in Navsari, Gujarat, India, where his grandfather, Dayaram Fakir, was born during British colonial rule.

He said like many Gujarati of the time, Dayaram believed there were better opportunities beyond India and was willing to take enormous risks to secure a better future. After travelling to Zanzibar in East Africa in 1923, Dayaram heard stories from fellow traders about Fiji.

“They spoke about a place where opportunities were beginning to emerge after the indenture system had ended.”

He said those conversations convinced his grandfather to return to India before boarding a ship from Calcutta for Fiji.

The two-month voyage across the ocean was long and difficult.

“There was very little comfort. Passengers travelled under harsh conditions with limited food, but despite all that, he never turned back.”

After arriving in Fiji in 1923, Dayaram settled in Nausori, on Viti Levu where he earned a living as a tailor, travelling from settlement to settlement selling clothing.

Mr Dayaram said his grandfather’s skills quickly became recognised.

“He was even given a block of land in exchange for sewing a suit. That tells you how valuable tailoring was during those days.”

While living in Nausori, Dayaram frequently listened to traders from Labasa, Vanua Levu talking about Taveuni.

Mr Dayaram said they described an island where former indentured labourers had established farms and where Catholic missionaries in Wairiki required traders who could provide groceries, clothing and other essential supplies.

Realising there was an opportunity to serve the growing community, Dayaram decided to move once again.

“He saw opportunity where others only saw isolation.”

In 1926, he arrived in Taveuni and established Dayaram Store, Tailors and Drapers, which later became Meridian Store.

Initially operating under a sublease, he later secured land from Sepo Dalo of the Mataitoga clan in Navarada.

He said his grandfather became the first Gujarati entrepreneur to establish a business on Taveuni. However, life on the island was far from easy.

His wife, Diwari Ben Fakir, remained in India, leaving him to build the business entirely on his own.

“There was no electricity, no piped water and supplies only arrived once a month. Imagine running a business under those conditions.”

Despite the hardships, Dayaram slowly earned the confidence of customers in Wairiki and neighbouring communities.

He said trust became the foundation upon which the business was built.

“People returned because they knew they would be treated fairly.”

Four years later, Dayaram’s only son, Ambaram Dayaram, arrived in Fiji at the age of 12 after another two-month voyage from India.

He quickly learnt tailoring, bookkeeping, customer service and copra trading while working alongside his father.

“By the time he was a teenager, he was already helping to manage many aspects of the business,” Mr Dayaram said.

In 1935, Ambaram returned to India to marry Narbada Ben before returning to Fiji.

World War II soon separated the young family. He said restrictions on sea travel meant Ambaram was unable to reunite with his wife and daughter for almost 10 years.

item.title: WEDDING BELLS | Charming wedding packages
item.description: SET among lush tropical gardens and Fiji’s largest orchid farm, South Sea Orchids in Nasau, Nadi offers a unique outdoor venue for weddings, receptions, engagements, and other special celebrations. Whether you’re planning an intimate ceremony or a larger gathering, their spacious grounds provide a naturally beautiful setting surrounded by orchids, tropical plants, open lawns, and peaceful countrys
item.content:encoded:
SET among lush tropical gardens and Fiji’s largest orchid farm, South Sea Orchids in Nasau, Nadi offers a unique outdoor venue for weddings, receptions, engagements, and other special celebrations.

Whether you’re planning an intimate ceremony or a larger gathering, their spacious grounds provide a naturally beautiful setting surrounded by orchids, tropical plants, open lawns, and peaceful countryside.

Every corner of the farm offers a picturesque backdrop, making it ideal for both your ceremony and wedding photography.

The venue can accommodate everything from small private ceremonies to celebrations of over 200 guests, with plenty of open space to create a layout that suits your wedding style.

Choose from garden ceremonies, outdoor receptions, or a combination of both, all within the one location.

They offer a range of wedding packages designed to suit different styles, guest numbers, and budgets.

If you have something specific in mind, they would be happy to work with you to create a package that fits your vision.

Their catering options are flexible, giving you the freedom to choose a menu that best suits your celebration. Whether you prefer a formal sit-down meal, a buffet, or light refreshments, they’ll work with you to ensure your guests are well looked after even if you prefer to self-cater.

To make planning even easier, they also offer décor hire, allowing you to transform the venue to match your chosen theme or colour palette.

From elegant and timeless to modern and rustic, their team can help create a setting that complements the natural beauty of the farm.

The farm features beautiful orchid gardens, mature tropical trees, open lawns, and a picturesque water lily pond, providing a variety of stunning locations for your wedding photographs throughout the day.

For couples or guests travelling from out of town, the South Sea Orchids Farmstead offers comfortable on-site accommodation, making it easy to relax before and after the celebration.

Conveniently located just minutes from Nadi International Airport, South Sea Orchids is easily accessible for both local and overseas guests.

With ample parking, flexible venue options, experienced staff, and a beautiful natural setting, everything you need for your special day is in one place.

Contact them today to arrange a viewing and discover how South Sea Orchids can help bring your wedding plans to life.

The venue can accommodate everything from small private ceremonies to celebrations of over 200 guests.

South Sea Orchids Farmstead offers comfortable on-site accommodation, making it easy to relax before and after the celebration. Pictures: SUPPLIED

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: WEDDING BELLS | Your dream wedding, right here in Fiji
item.description: EVERY couple deserves a wedding that reflects their love story, and at Shangri-La Yanuca Island, Fiji, those moments come to life against the breathtaking backdrop of Fiji’s iconic Coral Coast. Whether you’re envisioning an intimate beachfront ceremony, a romantic garden celebration or a grand reception surrounded by family and friends, our dedicated Weddings team works closely with you to bring y
item.content:encoded:
EVERY couple deserves a wedding that reflects their love story, and at Shangri-La Yanuca Island, Fiji, those moments come to life against the breathtaking backdrop of Fiji’s iconic Coral Coast.

Whether you’re envisioning an intimate beachfront ceremony, a romantic garden celebration or a grand reception surrounded by family and friends, our dedicated Weddings team works closely with you to bring your vision to life.

Backed by some of Fiji’s most experienced wedding planners and an exceptional selection of venues, every details thoughtfully tailored to create a celebration that is uniquely yours.

Enjoy the convenience of a fully integrated private island experience, where the seclusion and exclusivity of a 5-star resort are complemented by seamless access to Fiji’s mainland via a private causeway.

From elegant ceremony and reception spaces to luxurious accommodation, exceptional dining, rejuvenating spa experiences, and unforgettable island activities, every element of your wedding celebration is thoughtfully curated within one breathtaking destination.

For couples seeking a seamless, all-in-one celebration, our Tagimocia Deluxe Wedding Package includes a dedicated wedding coordinator, beautifully styled ceremony, traditional Fijian cultural touches, elegant reception, luxurious accommodation, romantic dining and indulgent spa and honeymoon inclusions.

Every detail is thoughtfully curated to create an effortless celebration from the moment you say “I do” to the beginning of your honeymoon.

Our private island setting offers endless opportunities for stunning wedding photography, while authentic cultural touches, including traditional warriors, Meke performances and local cultural experiences, create meaningful memories that will last a lifetime.

The celebration doesn’t have to end after the vows.

Many couples choose to extend the occasion into a weekend getaway with loved ones, enjoying the resort’s restaurants, pools, water sports, golf course, CHI.

The Spa, and the exclusive Adults-Only Reef Wing for aromantic honeymoon without ever leaving the island.

Whether you’re planning months in advance or organising an intimate celebration, our experienced Weddings team is here to guide you every step of the way, making the journey as enjoyable as the day itself.

Begin your forever togetherness beauty, warmth and hospitality of Fiji’s most iconic wedding destination.

Chaple at Shangri.La Yanuca Island Fiji.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
